items.title: Why use a urine osmolality threshold of at least 750 mOsm/kg
items.text: Thresholds of 600–750 mOsm/kg can miss partial DI; clinical practice shows patients subsequently diagnosed with partial DI whose initial urine osmolality fell between these values.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "Thus, it is advised to use a urine osmolality threshold of at least 750 mOsm/kg to assess the possibility of DI when there is clinical suspicion for disease."

items.title: How to confirm DI without water deprivation when plasma is already hyperosmolar
items.text: When simultaneous plasma osmolality exceeds 300 mOsm/kg and urine osmolality is below 300 mOsm/kg after excluding other causes of polyuria, DI is confirmed—formal water deprivation is unnecessary and desmopressin trial plus MRI can proceed.
items.reference: Central DI: "In children with polyuria and polydipsia, a plasma osmolality exceeding 300 mOsm/kg, reflecting hyperosmolality, with a simultaneous urine osmolality less than 300 mOsm/kg confirms the diagnosis of DI."

items.title: Non-DI causes of polyuria in children
items.text: Before pursuing central DI, exclude hyperglycemia, thyrotoxicosis, hypercalcemia, hypokalemia, protein malnutrition, lithium, and psychogenic polydipsia—the most common mimic of central DI.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "In addition to hyperglycemia, other conditions such as thyrotoxicosis, hypercalcemia, and hypokalemia can lead to polyuria."

items.title: Why MRI is mandatory after central DI is established
items.text: Once central DI is confirmed and the etiology is unknown, pituitary MRI is mandatory; the pediatric endocrinologist must decide when to repeat imaging and whether CNS biopsy is required.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "After establishing a diagnosis of central DI, MRI of the pituitary is mandatory if the etiology is not known."

items.title: How to surveil central DI with pituitary stalk thickening
items.text: Measure tumor markers and anterior pituitary hormones at presentation; repeat pituitary MRI and markers every 3 months for 1–2 years, then yearly for 2 years. CSF tumor markers (hCG, α-fetoprotein) are more sensitive than serum when germ-cell tumor is suspected.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "Pituitary MRI and measurement of serum tumor markers and anterior pituitary hormones should be performed every 3 months for 1 to 2 years, then yearly for 2 years."

items.title: Ectopic posterior pituitary bright spot on MRI
items.text: An ectopic posterior pituitary bright spot on T1-weighted images usually indicates the child does not have central DI but is at risk for anterior pituitary hormone deficiencies.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "A child who has an ectopic posterior pituitary gland recognized on T1-weighted images as an ectopic posterior pituitary bright spot does not typically have central DI but is at risk for anterior pituitary hormone deficiencies."

items.title: Why psychogenic polydipsia is the key mimic
items.text: Psychogenic polydipsia is the most common state that must be distinguished from central DI; severe cases can cause hyponatremic seizures, while persistently low or low-normal serum sodium without hypernatremia raises suspicion for psychiatric polydipsia.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "Psychogenic polydipsia is the most common state that must be distinguished from central DI."

items.title: How to choose desmopressin formulation in children
items.text: Desmopressin remains mainstay therapy since the 1970s; oral, intranasal, and subcutaneous formulations require individualized dose and schedule titration. Adverse effects include fluid retention and hyponatremia.
items.reference: Treatment of the Child With Central DI: "Desmopressin is available for outpatient therapy as a pill, nasal spray, or subcutaneous injection. The adverse effects of desmopressin include fluid retention and hyponatremia."

items.title: Triphasic DI/SIADH/DI response after pituitary surgery
items.text: Following complete stalk resection—common after craniopharyngioma surgery—children may show a well-described triphasic pattern of DI, then SIADH, then DI again in the immediate postoperative period.
items.reference: Central DI Following Neurosurgery: The Triphasic Response: "A well-described triphasic response of DI/SIADH/DI is seen in the immediate postoperative period following complete stalk resection, as observed in many children following surgery for craniopharyngioma."

items.title: How to manage central DI in infants
items.text: Infants cannot communicate thirst; fluids alone or thiazide diuretics with low solute intake may suffice. If desmopressin is used, choose a low dose and monitor closely for hyponatremia.
items.reference: The Infant With Central DI: "Often, fluids alone are used to treat DI, although adding a thiazide class of diuretics with a low solute intake can be effective."

items.title: Autosomal dominant central DI and AVP gene variants
items.text: Genetic central DI typically shows absent posterior bright spot with normal stalk size; more than 55 pathogenic AVP-peptide variants are described, most autosomal dominant, reducing AVP biological activity.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "More than 55 pathogenic variants that cause central DI have been described; most are associated with autosomal dominant inheritance."

items.title: Why partial or evolving DI requires prolonged testing
items.text: Patients with partial or evolving central DI may have reassuringly normal initial labs and require prolonged water deprivation to prove DI—premature reassurance can delay diagnosis.
items.reference: Summary: "those with partial or evolving central DI can have reassuringly normal laboratory results and may require prolonged water deprivation to prove there is DI."

items.question: A child has serum sodium 152 mEq/L, glucose 80 mg/dL, BUN 8 mg/dL, and urine osmolality 193 mOsm/kg. Other causes of polyuria are excluded. What is the best next step?
options: Order copeptin to establish DI of any cause
Perform inpatient water-deprivation testing
Initiate desmopressin therapy
Defer all therapy until formal osmolality is measured
items.explanation: Calculated serum osmolality already exceeds 300 mOsm/kg with dilute urine—DI is established. A desmopressin trial (with MRI if central) is appropriate without water deprivation.
items.reference: Case 2: "Answer: C) Therapy with desmopressin can be initiated"

items.question: A 14-year-old boy has water-deprivation–confirmed central DI. MRI shows absent posterior bright spot and infundibular thickening without mass; serum hCG and α-fetoprotein are negative. He is Tanner 4 with 16-mL testes, no headaches or vision changes. What is true?
options: Absent bright spot confirms CNS malignancy risk
Pubertal findings indicate stalled puberty
Cerebrospinal fluid should be sampled for tumor markers
Annual MRI for 5 years is sufficient surveillance
items.explanation: Thickened stalk raises risk for infiltrative disease; CSF tumor markers are more sensitive than serum for occult germ-cell tumors. MRI should be every 3 months initially, not yearly.
items.reference: Case 3: "Answer: C) Cerebrospinal fluid sample should be obtained"

items.question: A 4-year-old drinks from puddles and bathroom faucets with new enuresis and mild dehydration. Sodium 144 mEq/L, glucose 72 mg/dL, urine osmolality 184 mOsm/kg. What is true?
options: High copeptin would establish central DI
Inpatient water-deprivation study is indicated
MRI should precede all other testing
Desmopressin should be started immediately
items.explanation: Hyperosmolality is not yet established; inpatient water deprivation is needed before MRI or desmopressin when CNS symptoms are absent.
items.reference: Case 4: "Answer: B) A water-deprivation study is indicated and should be done on an inpatient basis"

items.question: Which paired laboratory findings best excludes diabetes insipidus?
options: Serum osmolality <270 mOsm/kg with maximally concentrated urine
Serum osmolality >300 mOsm/kg with urine osmolality <300 mOsm/kg
Serum sodium >145 mEq/L with urine output >4 mL/kg/h
Normal glucose with urine osmolality 400 mOsm/kg
items.explanation: Low serum osmolality with maximally concentrated urine excludes DI; hyperosmolality with dilute urine confirms it.
items.reference: Central DI: "if a simultaneous serum osmolality is less than 270 mOsm/kg and the urine osmolality is maximally concentrated, the diagnosis of DI is excluded."

items.question: In children with central DI and stalk thickening, an infundibular diameter greater than 4.5 mm correlates with what outcome?
options: Resolution of hypophysitis within 6 months
Greater likelihood of multiple anterior pituitary hormone deficiencies
Definitive diagnosis of germ-cell tumor
No need for repeat MRI
items.explanation: Stalk thickness >4.5 mm correlates with higher risk of evolving panhypopituitarism.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "an infundibular stalk greater than 4.5 mm in thickness was correlated with a greater likelihood of developing multiple anterior pituitary hormone deficiencies."

items.question: A child with central DI and thickened stalk has no bone lesions of Langerhans cell histiocytosis. What should be obtained at initial presentation?
options: Immediate pituitary biopsy
Serum and CSF quantitative hCG and α-fetoprotein
Empiric chemotherapy
PET scan before any biochemical testing
items.explanation: Tumor markers in serum and CSF assess for CNS germ-cell tumor when LCH is not evident.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "serum and cerebrospinal fluid tumor markers for quantitative hCG and α-fetoprotein should be obtained at initial presentation to assess for the possibility of a central nervous system germ-cell tumor."

items.question: A child has new central DI, normal pituitary stalk on MRI, and no CNS red-flag symptoms. What MRI schedule is recommended?
options: No repeat imaging ever needed
Repeat MRI in 3–6 months, then every 6–12 months for 2 years
Monthly MRI for 5 years
MRI only if desmopressin dose increases
items.explanation: Even a normal stalk does not exclude occult pathology; structured repeat imaging is advised.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "it is recommended to perform a repeated MRI in 3 to 6 months, then every 6 to 12 months for the next 2 years."

items.question: Which imaging feature most strongly suggests craniopharyngioma in a sellar/suprasellar mass causing central DI?
options: Absent posterior bright spot alone
Calcifications on CT
Thickened infundibulum without mass
Ectopic posterior pituitary
items.explanation: Calcifications strongly suggest craniopharyngioma; CT detects them more reliably than MRI.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "Calcifications strongly suggest craniopharyngioma. CT is more reliable than MRI in detecting calcifications."

items.question: A child on lithium presents with polyuria. What is the mechanism?
options: Central AVP deficiency from hypothalamic injury
Reduced renal responsiveness to AVP (nephrogenic pattern)
Osmotic diuresis from hyperglycemia
Primary polydipsia from psychiatric disease
items.explanation: Lithium reduces renal responsiveness to AVP, causing nephrogenic concentrating defects.
items.reference: Polyuria: Differential Diagnosis: "Lithium is unique because it reduces the renal responsiveness to AVP, leading to defects in urinary concentrating capacity."

items.question: Regarding copeptin measurement in pediatric DI workup, which statement is most accurate?
options: Low copeptin after hypertonic saline confirms nephrogenic DI
Copeptin has been fully validated as a water-deprivation substitute in children
Copeptin may help identify central DI but has not been studied in pediatric patients
High copeptin in hyperosmolality confirms central DI
items.explanation: Copeptin after hypertonic saline is promising but not yet studied in children; low copeptin in hyperosmolality would suggest central, not nephrogenic, DI.
items.reference: The Child With Polydipsia and Polyuria: "Copeptin measurement after infusion of hypertonic saline may be an alternative approach to water-deprivation testing in diagnosing DI, but this has not been studied in pediatric patients."

items.question: Which combination supports formal DI diagnosis after excluding other polyuria causes?
options: Serum sodium >145 mEq/L, urine osmolality <300 mOsm/kg, urine output >4 mL/kg/h
Serum sodium <136 mEq/L with dilute urine
Urine osmolality >750 mOsm/kg with polydipsia
Normal serum osmolality with concentrated urine
items.explanation: Hypernatremia/hyperosmolality with dilute urine and high urine output defines DI once mimics are excluded.
items.reference: Central DI: "DI is diagnosed when the serum sodium concentration exceeds 145 mEq/L (>145 mmol/L), urine osmolality is less than 300 mOsm/kg, urine output is persistently greater than 4 mL/kg per h, and other causes of polyuria have been excluded."

items.question: Approximately what proportion of children with central DI show thickened pituitary stalk on MRI, and what fraction of those may have germ-cell tumor?
options: One-third thickened; ~17% germ-cell tumor
Two-thirds thickened; ~50% germ-cell tumor
10% thickened; nearly all Langerhans cell histiocytosis
All have thickened stalk; biopsy is always required
items.explanation: About one-third have stalk thickening; among those, ~17% germ-cell tumor and ~17% LCH in one cited series.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "MRI in children with central DI shows a thickened stalk in approximately one-third of cases. Of the children with a thickened stalk, one study showed that 17% were eventually diagnosed with a germ-cell tumor and 17% were diagnosed with Langerhans cell histiocytosis."

items.question: Which history finding most strongly suggests pathologic thirst rather than benign increased fluid intake?
options: Drinking extra water during sports
Drinking from toilet bowls, puddles, or others' bottles
Using a school water fountain at lunch
Parent-reported normal urine frequency
items.explanation: Drinking from unusual sources signals abnormal thirst warranting DI evaluation.
items.reference: The Child With Polydipsia and Polyuria: "Drinking from unusual sources such as puddles, bathtub faucets, the toilet, or other people's drinks suggests abnormal thirst."

items.statement: A formal water-deprivation study is always required before diagnosing diabetes insipidus in children.
items.explanation: When plasma osmolality already exceeds 300 mOsm/kg with urine osmolality below 300 mOsm/kg, DI is confirmed without water deprivation.
items.reference: Case 2: "The main point of this vignette is to show that sometimes water-deprivation study (Answer B) is not needed, as the diagnosis is already established."

items.statement: An absent posterior pituitary bright spot is specific for central nervous system malignancy.
items.explanation: The absent bright spot is seen in all causes of central DI, not specific to tumor.
items.reference: Case 3: "The absent bright spot is seen in all causes of central DI, so it is not a specific finding for a tumor etiology"

items.statement: Psychogenic polydipsia is the most common condition that must be distinguished from central DI.
items.explanation: Explicitly stated in main conclusions.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "Psychogenic polydipsia is the most common state that must be distinguished from central DI."

items.statement: Children with central DI and stalk thickening should have pituitary MRI every 3 months for the first 1–2 years.
items.explanation: Recommended surveillance interval for thickened stalk.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "Pituitary MRI and measurement of serum tumor markers and anterior pituitary hormones should be performed every 3 months for 1 to 2 years, then yearly for 2 years."

items.statement: An ectopic posterior pituitary bright spot typically indicates central DI.
items.explanation: Ectopic bright spot usually means no central DI but risk of anterior pituitary deficiency.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "does not typically have central DI but is at risk for anterior pituitary hormone deficiencies."

items.statement: Hypercalcemia can impair renal concentrating capacity and cause polyuria.
items.explanation: Listed among endocrine polyuria mimics.
items.reference: Polyuria: Differential Diagnosis: "Hypercalcemia, regardless of etiology, can produce deficits in renal concentrating capacity."

items.statement: Desmopressin therapy carries risk of hyponatremia from fluid retention.
items.explanation: Fluid retention and hyponatremia are recognized adverse effects.
items.reference: Treatment of the Child With Central DI: "The adverse effects of desmopressin include fluid retention and hyponatremia."

items.statement: Idiopathic (likely hypophysitis), tumor, histiocytosis, germ-cell tumors, structural defects, and congenital genetic defects are the most common causes of central DI.
items.explanation: Listed as most common etiologies after diagnosis.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "The most common causes are idiopathic (likely hypophysitis), tumor, histiocytosis, germ-cell tumors, structural defects, and congenital (genetic) defects."

items.assertion: Assertion: A urine osmolality threshold of at least 750 mOsm/kg should be used when clinical suspicion for DI is high.
items.reason: Reason: Some patients initially between 600 and 750 mOsm/kg are later diagnosed with partial DI.
items.explanation: Both true and causally linked.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "we have cared for patients who were subsequently diagnosed with partial DI whose initial urine osmolality was between these values."

items.assertion: Assertion: Pituitary MRI is mandatory after central DI is established when etiology is unknown.
items.reason: Reason: MRI always reveals the diagnosis on first scan.
items.explanation: Assertion true; initial MRI often does not reveal etiology—reason false.
items.reference: Barriers to Optimal Practice: "One of the challenges in establishing the etiology of central DI is that the diagnosis is usually not known after the initial MRI."

items.assertion: Assertion: CSF tumor markers may be more sensitive than serum for occult germ-cell tumors in central DI.
items.reason: Reason: Germ-cell tumors never secrete hCG or α-fetoprotein into CSF.
items.explanation: Assertion true per case discussion; reason false.
items.reference: Case 3: "cerebrospinal fluid is more sensitive than serum samples."

items.assertion: Assertion: AVP acts on V2 receptors in renal collecting ducts to insert aquaporin-2 channels.
items.reason: Reason: AVP binding increases collecting duct water permeability via cAMP signaling.
items.explanation: Both true and mechanistically linked.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "AVP binds to the AVP V2 receptors in the collecting ducts of the renal tubules. Through a cyclic AMP signal transduction mechanism, this results in activation, transport, and insertion of aquaporin-2 water channels"

items.assertion: Assertion: Children after complete stalk resection may show DI, then SIADH, then DI again.
items.reason: Reason: This triphasic pattern never occurs after craniopharyngioma surgery.
items.explanation: Assertion describes classic postoperative course; reason false.
items.reference: Central DI Following Neurosurgery: The Triphasic Response: "A well-described triphasic response of DI/SIADH/DI is seen in the immediate postoperative period following complete stalk resection"

items.assertion: Assertion: Neonatal central DI is particularly difficult because infants cannot communicate thirst.
items.reason: Reason: Infants reliably self-regulate free water intake without caregiver input.
items.explanation: Assertion true; infants depend on caregivers—reason false.
items.reference: The Infant With Central DI: "Management of neonatal DI is particularly difficult, as infants cannot communicate thirst and are dependent on caretakers for providing fluids."

items.assertion: Assertion: Hypernatremia without polyuria often reflects hypernatremic dehydration rather than DI.
items.reason: Reason: All hypernatremia in children is due to central DI.
items.explanation: Assertion true for dehydration settings; reason overgeneralizes.
items.reference: Hypernatremia (Hyperosmolality): "Hypernatremia in the absence of polyuria most often reflects hypernatremic dehydration"

items.assertion: Assertion: Resolution of pituitary stalk thickening is more often associated with lymphocytic hypophysitis.
items.reason: Reason: Stalk thickening never resolves in any condition.
items.explanation: Assertion true; LCH lesions can also change size—reason false.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "Resolution of pituitary stalk thickening is more often associated with lymphocytic hypophysitis and confers a positive prognosis."
